description: Explains how marimo SQL cells execute queries, return dataframe-like results, discover database connections and schemas, and connect notebook data workflows to external data sources.
document:
marimo treats SQL as part of the same reactive notebook model as Python rather than as a separate file or hidden execution layer. A SQL cell is syntactic sugar for a Python cell that calls `mo.sql`, so the notebook remains a pure Python script while users can write SQL directly in the editor. The SQL text is represented as a formatted Python string, which lets queries depend on UI controls and other Python values. Results can be assigned to a visible variable and then reused by later Python or SQL cells; a private underscored variable is intentionally not available as a public dependency for other cells.

The default query path is built around DuckDB. When no custom engine is passed, the SQL helper requires the DuckDB and parsing dependencies, executes against an in-memory DuckDB connection, and lets queries reference local dataframe variables from the notebook namespace. The user-facing guide presents this as one workflow: query Pandas or Polars dataframes, files, HTTP endpoints, local databases, and DuckDB-supported sources, then get a result back in Python. The configured SQL output type decides whether the result stays native and lazy, becomes a lazy or eager Polars dataframe, becomes a Pandas dataframe, or is chosen automatically from installed packages. The docs recommend native output for large datasets because it avoids eagerly loading the full result and makes chained SQL cells more efficient.

The runtime display path is cautious with large or special results. `sql` returns early for empty input, wraps parse failures in a marimo SQL exception, and can honor a default row limit from the environment when the query itself did not include a limit. DuckDB explain output is shown as plain text so its formatting is preserved. Lazy dataframe-like results are displayed through a table view that preloads only the first page, while cursor-style results from database libraries are converted to metadata suitable for display.

Custom database access is organized around engine detection. The SQL guide says users can connect through the editor UI for common databases or define a Python connection object in a cell. Internally, marimo checks notebook variables against the supported engine classes in `get_engines_from_variables`, then wraps compatible values as query engines or catalog-aware connections. The important contract is that compatible Python connection variables become selectable SQL connections in the notebook.

The Data Sources panel uses a related but broader introspection path. `engine_to_data_source_connection` turns a detected connection into a display model with its source, dialect, display name, default database and schema when available, and discovered databases. Engines that implement the catalog interface can expose schemas, tables, and columns; when introspection fails, marimo logs a warning and still returns a connection entry rather than crashing the notebook. Datasource configuration controls how aggressively schemas, tables, and columns are discovered, with conservative defaults for performance on large warehouses. DuckDB has a dedicated discovery implementation that groups visible tables by database and schema, skips internal system tables, maps database column types into marimo's simpler data types, and falls back to alternate catalog queries when needed.

Dataframe integration supplies the local side of the same experience. `get_datasets_from_variables` scans notebook variables with the table-manager abstraction and produces lightweight table metadata for dataframe-like values: variable name, row and column counts when available, column types, external types, and sample values. The dataframe guide explains how those values are displayed in rich interactive tables, transformed through `mo.ui.dataframe`, filtered with normal UI elements, and selected through tables or charts with the selected rows returned to Python. SQL output preferences also appear in the dataframe docs, tying the dataframe viewer and SQL execution settings together.

The integration guides show that marimo does not require bespoke connectors for every data source. Because notebooks are Python, users can use the ordinary client libraries for cloud storage, BigQuery, Google Sheets, and similar services, authenticate with application default credentials or service-account environment variables, then expose results through marimo UI tables or dataframes. MotherDuck is closer to the SQL path: users attach the cloud database through DuckDB, authenticate in the browser or with an environment token, browse discovered tables in the Data Sources panel, and query them from SQL cells while downstream cells update reactively.
